description: This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images illustrating the microstructural evolution of 30Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr duplex low-density steel under different intercritical annealing conditions. Based on text extraction data, the images compare three critical samples: DP950-5 (annealed at 950°C for 5 min), DP1000-5 (1000°C for 5 min), and DP1050-1 (1050°C for 1 min). DP950-5 exhibits partially recrystallized austenite with high ferrite fractions (36% total, comprising 23.8% granular α and 12.1% banded δ ferrite), coarse Cr7C3 carbides at grain boundaries, and intergranular κ-carbides (2-3 μm). DP1000-5 shows fully recrystallized austenite (95.2% fraction, average grain size 7.40±5.13 μm) with reduced ferrite content (4.1% α and 9.3% δ) and high-density intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles (0.86±0.20 nm, 1.56% volume fraction). DP1050-1 displays larger recrystallized austenite grains (9.65±8.17 μm) with minimal ferrite (2.7% α and 4.6% δ) due to complete carbide dissolution. The images likely include EBSD phase maps or higher magnification views showing ferrite morphologies and crack propagation paths. These microstructural variations demonstrate how annealing parameters control recrystallization, phase transformation, and precipitation to achieve the optimized strength-ductility-density synergy.

This figure presents the phase evolution and precipitate characterization of a novel 30Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr duplex low-density steel annealed at 600–1050 °C. Panel (a) displays X-ray diffraction patterns revealing the temperature-dependent phase transformation: the cold-rolled (CR) structure contains austenite (FCC) and ferrite (BCC); upon annealing, β-Mn forms at 700–900 °C, while Cr7C3 carbides and κ-carbides appear at 950–1000 °C, and dissolve at 1050 °C. Panels (b) and (c) provide transmission electron microscopy evidence. Subfigure (b) shows DP950-5 (950 °C, 5 min) with Cr7C3 carbides precipitating at grain boundaries, confirmed by the [0001] zone axis SAED pattern. Subfigure (c) depicts DP1000-5 (1000 °C, 5 min), where ferrite and austenite coexist; high-resolution imaging identifies ultrafine intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles within austenite (radius 0.86 ± 0.20 nm, volume fraction 1.56 %), with corresponding SAED patterns confirming the austenite/κ-carbide orientation relationship. These results demonstrate that annealing at 1000 °C dissolves brittle intergranular carbides while precipitating strengthening intragranular nanoparticles, achieving an optimal microstructure.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of microstructural evolution in a 30Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr duplex low-density steel subjected to various annealing treatments. The upper row (a1-f1) displays phase mappings (PH) distinguishing FCC austenite (cyan) and BCC ferrite (red), while the lower row (a2-f2) shows grain-reference-orientation-deviation (GROD) mappings of austenite with dark lines indicating high-angle grain boundaries (>15°). The six columns represent: cold-rolled condition (CR), annealing at 950°C for 1 min (DP950-1) and 5 min (DP950-5), 1000°C for 1 min (DP1000-1) and 5 min (DP1000-5), and 1050°C for 1 min (DP1050-1). Key observations reveal that CR exhibits shear bands in deformed austenite. DP950-5 shows the highest ferrite fraction (~36%) with coarse granular α and banded δ ferrite, partial recrystallization (54.8%), and remaining shear bands. Increasing annealing temperature to 1000°C and above (DP1000-1, DP1000-5, DP1050-1) promotes complete recrystallization with annealing twin formation, reduced ferrite content, and grain growth from 5.13±4.74 μm (DP1000-1) to 9.65±8.17 μm (DP1050-1). The GROD maps indicate that yellow regions (0°-2° misorientation) correspond to recrystallized grains, while red/pink regions (2°-180°) represent deformed structures. The figure demonstrates that annealing at 1000°C achieves optimal microstructure with fully recrystallized austenite matrix, fine dispersed ferrite bands, and minimized carbides, supporting the superior strength-ductility combination reported for DP1000-5.

This figure presents the mechanical property characterization of a novel 30Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr duplex low-density steel under various annealing conditions. Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves for six processing variants: DP950-1, DP950-5 (950°C for 1 and 5 min), DP1000-1, DP1000-5 (1000°C for 1 and 5 min), DP1050-1 (1050°C for 1 min), and DP1000-5-TD (transverse direction). The curves reveal that DP1000-5 achieves an optimal balance with UTS ~1250 MPa and total elongation ~41.4%, while DP1050-1 exhibits higher ductility (~47.5%) but lower strength. Specimens annealed at 950°C show limited ductility (~5%), and DP1000-5-TD demonstrates reduced elongation (~18.1%) compared to its rolling direction counterpart due to oriented δ-ferrite bands. Panel (b) compares specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) versus total elongation (TE) against benchmark materials including 2000-series Al alloys, Ti6Al4V, austenitic steels, and conventional duplex steels. The contour lines indicate SUTS×TE products ranging from 2 to 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%. DP1000-5 and DP1050-1 surpass 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%, outperforming existing lightweight alloys. This unprecedented combination stems from optimized recrystallized austenite matrix, retained δ-ferrite bands for crack deflection, and intragranular κ-carbide nanoprecipitates.

Figure 4 presents combined SEM and EBSD characterization revealing crack initiation and propagation mechanisms in duplex low-density steel specimens DP1000–5 (annealed at 1000 °C for 5 min and tested along rolling direction) and DP1000–5-TD (tested along transverse direction). Subfigures (a1) and (a2) display the rolling-direction specimen: SEM image (a1) shows crack nucleation at α/γ interfaces, propagation along the normal direction (ND), and deflection along the rolling direction (RD) upon encountering δ-ferrite bands parallel to RD, while corresponding EBSD phase map (a2) distinguishes FCC austenite (cyan) and BCC ferrite (red), confirming that cracks interact with soft δ-ferrite bands. Subfigures (b1) and (b2) illustrate the transverse-direction specimen: SEM image (b1) indicates crack nucleation at δ/γ interfaces with propagation along the transverse direction, and EBSD map (b2) shows δ-ferrite bands oriented perpendicular to the tensile axis. The figure demonstrates that when δ-ferrite bands are parallel to the tensile direction, cracks are effectively deflected and blunted within the soft ferrite, contributing to high ductility (41.4% total elongation), whereas perpendicular orientation causes high decohesive stress at δ/γ interfaces, leading to rapid crack connection across thickness and reduced ductility (18.1% total elongation).
